sources.name: Animal Politico
sources.focus: Investigative reporting on security, corruption, politics. Cartel violence, forced disappearances, government accountability. Paraphernalia series on drug policy.
sources.admiralty: A2 — Established independent outlet with investigative track record
sources.originality: High — original investigative reporting, not aggregation
sources.track_record: Strong — broke AMLO-era corruption stories, Ayotzinapa coverage

sources.name: Reforma
sources.focus: Leading national newspaper. Security, politics, economy, energy. Strong Mexico City and national coverage.
sources.admiralty: A2 — Established major national newspaper

sources.name: El Universal
sources.focus: Mexico's oldest newspaper. National security, politics, US-Mexico relations, energy policy.
sources.admiralty: A2 — Oldest continuously publishing newspaper in Mexico

sources.name: Milenio
sources.focus: National daily with strong security and cartel coverage. Grupo Milenio network across states. Good regional bureaus.
sources.admiralty: A2 — Major national newspaper with regional network

sources.name: El Financiero
sources.focus: Mexico's leading financial newspaper. Peso, Banxico, sovereign debt, FDI, nearshoring, economic policy, markets.
sources.admiralty: A2 — Mexico's premier financial daily

sources.name: El Economista
sources.focus: Financial and economic news. Sector analysis, trade, nearshoring, FDI flows, industrial policy.
sources.admiralty: B2 — Established financial daily, less investigative depth than El Financiero

sources.name: Aristegui Noticias
sources.focus: Investigative journalism platform led by Carmen Aristegui. Corruption investigations, security, human rights, political scandals.
sources.admiralty: A2 — High-integrity investigative outlet

sources.name: Proceso
sources.focus: Leading investigative weekly. Deep-dive political analysis, cartel investigations, government corruption. Weekly magazine format.
sources.admiralty: A2 — Storied investigative weekly, 50+ year track record

sources.name: La Jornada
sources.focus: Left-leaning national daily. Strong cultural and human rights coverage. Sheinbaum-friendly editorial line but useful for government narrative tracking.
sources.admiralty: B3 — Strong editorial bias toward Morena/government. Use for government narrative, cross-reference security claims.

sources.name: El País México
sources.focus: El País Mexico edition. Strong coverage of Mexican politics, security, US-Mexico relations from international perspective.
sources.admiralty: A2 — Spanish global newspaper, strong Mexico bureau

sources.name: Riodoce (Sinaloa)
sources.focus: Sinaloa-focused independent newspaper. Chapitos/Menos faction warfare, Sinaloa cartel dynamics, Culiacán security. Critical local coverage.
sources.admiralty: A2 — Independent regional paper with deep Sinaloa institutional knowledge
sources.note: Essential for Sinaloa cartel ground truth. Local reporters embedded in cartel territory.

sources.name: Pie de Página
sources.focus: Independent investigative journalism collective. Forced disappearances, cartel violence, human rights, environmental crime.
sources.admiralty: A2 — Rigorous independent collective, MacArthur-supported

sources.name: A dónde van los desaparecidos
sources.focus: Family-led tracking of Mexico's 100k+ disappeared. Mapping forced disappearances, mass graves, search brigades. Essential ground-truth source on disappearance crisis.
sources.admiralty: B3 — Citizen-led initiative, emotionally invested but valuable ground data

sources.name: Quinto Elemento Lab
sources.focus: Data-driven investigative journalism. Security, violence mapping, cartel finances, corruption quantified. MacArthur Foundation-supported.
sources.admiralty: A2 — Professional data journalism lab with strong methodology

sources.name: Borderland Beat
sources.focus: Cartel news aggregator with English translations of Mexican media. User-edited but well-sourced. Factional dynamics, cartel messages (narcomantas), incidents.
sources.admiralty: C3 — User-edited site, source links to Mexican media. Cross-reference original reporting.

sources.name: InSight Crime Mexico
sources.focus: Deep-dive analysis of organized crime in Mexico. Cartel profiles, criminal economies, factional mapping, government response. Gold standard for OC analysis in Americas.
sources.admiralty: A1 — Authoritative, methodologically transparent think tank

sources.name: DOF (Diario Oficial de la Federación)
sources.focus: Mexico's official government gazette. Decrees, laws, regulations, judicial reforms, Pemex/CFE official acts. Essential for legal/regulatory monitoring.
sources.admiralty: A1 — Official primary source, government regulation

sources.name: SEDENA (Secretaría de la Defensa Nacional)
sources.focus: Mexican army press briefings, operational reports. Security operations, cartel engagements, military deployments. Official military narrative.
sources.admiralty: A2 — Official government source with institutional bias. Cross-reference with regional reporting.

sources.name: SEMAR (Secretaría de Marina)
sources.focus: Mexican navy press releases. Maritime security, port interdictions, drug seizures at sea, Gulf/ Pacific naval operations.
sources.admiralty: A2 — Official government source

sources.name: FGR (Fiscalía General de la República)
sources.focus: Federal Attorney General's office. High-profile arrests, extraditions, cartel prosecutions, asset seizures, organized crime indictments.
sources.admiralty: A2 — Official government source with political sensitivity

sources.name: SAT / Aduanas (Servicio de Administración Tributaria)
sources.focus: Mexican tax and customs authority. Fentanyl precursor seizures, trade data, customs enforcement, huachicoleo (fuel theft) interdiction statistics.
sources.admiralty: A1 — Official government statistical source

sources.name: Banxico (Banco de México)
sources.focus: Mexico's central bank. Monetary policy, interest rate decisions, inflation data, FX reserves, financial stability reports, GDP forecasts. Official economic data.
sources.admiralty: A1 — Autonomous central bank, authoritative
sources.frequency: Monetary policy every 6 weeks. Inflation 2x/month. Quarterly reports.

sources.name: Pemex Investor Relations
sources.focus: Pemex official IR. Production data, refinery utilization (Dos Bocas, Deer Park), debt profile, bond markets, fuel theft data, operational metrics.
sources.admiralty: A1 — Official corporate disclosure with SEC-equivalent reporting

sources.name: CFE (Comisión Federal de Electricidad)
sources.focus: Federal Electricity Commission. Energy generation, transmission, renewables integration, data center power, natural gas supply.
sources.admiralty: A2 — Official state utility, some data transparency

sources.name: CRE (Comisión Reguladora de Energía)
sources.focus: Energy Regulatory Commission. Hydrocarbon permits, fuel pricing, pipeline regulation, retail fuel market. Energy sector reform monitoring.
sources.admiralty: A2 — Official regulator, permit-level data

sources.name: CNH (Comisión Nacional de Hidrocarburos)
sources.focus: National Hydrocarbons Commission. Oil/gas exploration and production regulation, Pemex farmouts, upstream oversight.
sources.admiralty: A2 — Official regulator

sources.name: INEGI (Instituto Nacional de Estadística y Geografía)
sources.focus: National statistics and geography institute. Economic indicators, GDP, employment, violence statistics, homicide data, geographic/cartographic data. Authoritative data source.
sources.admiralty: A1 — Independent autonomous statistical agency, professional standards

sources.name: CBP (US Customs and Border Protection)
sources.focus: Southwest border encounter data, fentanyl seizures, migration statistics, operational updates. Official US border security metrics.
sources.admiralty: A1 — Official US government statistical source

sources.name: DEA (Drug Enforcement Administration)
sources.focus: DEA public releases, drug threat assessments, fentanyl enforcement operations. Annual National Drug Threat Assessment, Mexico-specific cartel reporting.
sources.admiralty: A2 — US law enforcement, politically shaped, but operationally authoritative
sources.frequency: Annual reports, press releases during operations

sources.name: OFAC (Office of Foreign Assets Control)
sources.focus: US Treasury sanctions designations. Cartel FTO/SDTO designations, kingpin act sanctions, entity/individual blacklisting. Essential for tracking US financial pressure on cartels.
sources.admiralty: A1 — Official US government sanctions list, primary source
sources.frequency: Irregular — designation announcements as they occur

sources.name: USTR (Office of the US Trade Representative)
sources.focus: USMCA 2026 review official documents, tariff announcements, trade negotiations. Essential source for USMCA timeline and US-Mexico trade policy.
sources.admiralty: A1 — Official US government trade policy source
sources.frequency: Press releases tied to USMCA review schedule

sources.name: State Department Mexico Desk
sources.focus: State Department press briefings, travel advisories, human rights reports, international narcotics control strategy, Mexico country reports.
sources.admiralty: A1 — Official US diplomatic source
sources.frequency: Travel advisories updated regularly, annual INCSR report

sources.name: FBI Houston / El Paso Field Offices
sources.focus: FBI press releases on Mexico-related operations. Transnational organized crime task forces, cartel prosecutions, extortion investigations.
sources.admiralty: A2 — US law enforcement, operation-specific releases

sources.name: DHS (Department of Homeland Security)
sources.focus: Border security, immigration enforcement, CISA advisories, counter-fentanyl operations, DHS IA strategic assessments.
sources.admiralty: A2 — Official US security agency

sources.name: Treasury FinCEN Advisories
sources.focus: Financial Crime Enforcement Network advisories on Mexico. Fentanyl trafficking financial flows, trade-based money laundering, virtual currency risks, real estate ML typologies.
sources.admiralty: A1 — Official US financial intelligence source
sources.frequency: As issued — tied to typology/methodology releases

sources.name: Wilson Center Mexico Institute
sources.focus: Leading US think tank on Mexico. US-Mexico relations, migration, border policy, energy, trade. Publications, event transcripts, policy briefs.
sources.admiralty: A2 — Well-respected bipartisan think tank, strong Mexico network

sources.name: Brookings Latin America Initiative
sources.focus: Brookings research on Latin America. Vanda Felbab-Brown's Mexico security work, drug policy, organized crime. High-quality academic-policy analysis.
sources.admiralty: A1 — Top-tier think tank, rigorous peer review
sources.note: Follow Vanda Felbab-Brown specifically — leading scholar on Mexico drug policy and organized crime

sources.name: RAND Corporation — Mexico/Latin America
sources.focus: RAND research on Mexico security, cartel dynamics, US-Mexico cooperation, security sector reform. Quantitative security analysis, scenario modeling.
sources.admiralty: A1 — Tier 1 think tank, rigorous methodology

sources.name: International Crisis Group — Mexico/Latin America
sources.focus: Crisis Group reporting on Mexico's conflict dynamics, cartel violence, mass disappearances, peacebuilding. Falko Ernst's Mexico work is essential reading.
sources.admiralty: A1 — Premier conflict resolution think tank, field-based research
sources.note: Follow Falko Ernst — Crisis Group's senior Mexico analyst, best-in-class ground reporting

sources.name: Atlantic Council — Latin America Center
sources.focus: US-Mexico security cooperation, energy integration, digital transformation in Mexico, nearshoring policy. Policy-focused analysis.
sources.admiralty: A2 — Policy-oriented think tank, strong network

sources.name: CSIS Americas Program
sources.focus: CSIS research on Western Hemisphere security, Mexico energy reform, USMCA, migration, defense cooperation. Strong quantitative output.
sources.admiralty: A1 — Top-tier policy think tank

sources.name: Colegio de México (Colmex)
sources.focus: Mexico's premier academic institution for social sciences. Security studies, political science, economics. Academic journals, working papers, conference proceedings.
sources.admiralty: A2 — Top-tier academic institution, peer-reviewed research

sources.name: CIDE (Centro de Investigación y Docencia Económicas)
sources.focus: Leading Mexican research center. Security economics, political science, regulatory policy, energy economics. High-quality quantitative social science.
sources.admiralty: A2 — Prestigious Mexican research institution

sources.name: Justice in Mexico (USD-JSD)
sources.focus: University of San Diego program tracking Mexican rule of law. Homicide tracking, judicial reform, human rights, cartel violence statistics. Systematic datasets.
sources.admiralty: A2 — Academic program with methodical data collection

sources.name: Lantia Intelligence (Eduardo Guerrero)
sources.focus: Mexico security risk consultancy run by Eduardo Guerrero. Cartel territorial mapping, violence forecasting, criminal governance analysis. Best-in-class Mexico security analysis.
sources.admiralty: A2 — Leading Mexico security consultancy, data-driven methodology
sources.paywall: Yes — paid consultancy, but public outputs available
sources.note: Eduardo Guerrero is Mexico's most cited security analyst. Essential methodological grounding.

sources.name: Kalshi — Mexico Prediction Contracts
sources.focus: US-regulated prediction market for Mexico-specific contracts: peso FX, Banxico rate path, Sheinbaum approval, USMCA outcomes
sources.admiralty: A1 — CFTC-regulated, real money, market-derived probabilities

sources.name: Polymarket — Mexico Prediction Contracts
sources.focus: Crypto prediction market for Mexico contracts: peso path, Sheinbaum policies, cartel violence metrics, election outcomes
sources.admiralty: B2 — Less regulated than Kalshi, but volume signals market attention

sources.name: Falko Ernst (ICG / X)
sources.focus: International Crisis Group Senior Analyst Mexico. Cartel dynamics, criminal governance, disappearance crisis. Best-in-class field reporting.
sources.admiralty: A1 — Top Mexico analyst, field-based research, ICG institutional backing

sources.name: Mike Vigil (ex-DEA / X/Substack)
sources.focus: Former DEA Chief of International Operations. Cartel operations, US-Mexico law enforcement, fentanyl trafficking. Insider perspective on US drug enforcement.
sources.admiralty: B3 — Former official with insider knowledge but retired perspective. Cross-reference with current sources.

sources.name: Vanda Felbab-Brown (Brookings / X)
sources.focus: Brookings Senior Fellow. Drug policy, organized crime, illicit economies, Mexico security policy. Leading academic voice on counternarcotics.
sources.admiralty: A1 — Rigorous Brookings-affiliated scholar with deep Mexico expertise

sources.name: Cecilia Farfán-Méndez (UCSD / Justice in Mexico)
sources.focus: UC San Diego researcher, Executive Director of Justice in Mexico. Judicial reform, drug policy, security sector reform. Methodical quantitative work.
sources.admiralty: A2 — Academic researcher with systematic data collection

sources.name: Eduardo Guerrero (Lantia / X)
sources.focus: Founder of Lantia Intelligence. Mexico's premier security risk analyst. Cartel territorial evolution, criminal governance metrics, violence forecasting.
sources.admiralty: A2 — Preeminent Mexico security analyst, data-driven methodology

sources.name: GZERO Media — Latin America
sources.focus: Eurasia Group's daily geopolitical newsletter. Good Latin America/Mexico coverage, USMCA analysis, Sheinbaum administration tracking.
sources.admiralty: A2 — Eurasia Group-linked, professional geopolitical analysis

sources.name: Border Report
sources.focus: US-Mexico border news and analysis. Border crossing data, cartel activity in border cities, customs enforcement, fentanyl seizures. Border-based reporting team.
sources.admiralty: B2 — Regional news outlet focused on border, less national depth

sources.name: Sistema Nacional de Seguridad Pública (SESNSP)
sources.focus: Official crime statistics database. Homicides, kidnappings, extortion, vehicle theft, femicides — monthly by state and municipality. Gold standard for Mexico crime data.
sources.admiralty: A2 — Official government statistical series, methodology concerns
sources.note: Methodology changes and political pressure on data are known issues. Cross-reference with INEGI and Justice in Mexico.

sources.name: El Blog del Narco
sources.focus: Cartel violence compilation and news aggregator. Graphic incident reporting, cartel messages, factional violence documentation. English-language accessibility.
sources.admiralty: C3 — User-sourced content, verify all claims against primary sources

sources.name: USMCA Monitoring — CBP Trade Data
sources.focus: US-Mexico trade data, USMCA utilization rates, rules of origin tracking. Essential for nearshoring and trade flow analysis.
sources.admiralty: A1 — Official US government trade statistics

sources.name: SECODAM (Secretaría de la Función Pública)
sources.focus: Mexico's public administration ministry. Corruption investigations, procurement oversight, conflict-of-interest declarations. Government accountability signals.
sources.admiralty: A2 — Official government anti-corruption body

sources.name: IEPRI (Universidad Iberoamericana) — Security Observatory
sources.focus: Iberoamericana University's security research institute. Peer-reviewed analysis of organized crime, violence, security policy. Academic rigor.
sources.admiralty: A2 — Respected academic institution

sources.name: Pemex.com.mx — Corporate Press
sources.focus: Pemex official press releases: production levels, refinery startups, fuel theft data (huachicoleo), partnerships, corruption scandals. Operational primary source.
sources.admiralty: A1 — Official corporate/government press office

sources.name: INVEST IN MEXICO / SE (Secretaría de Economía)
sources.focus: Mexico's Ministry of Economy. FDI data, nearshoring statistics, trade policy, USMCA implementation, investment promotion, special economic zones.
sources.admiralty: A1 — Official government ministry
sources.frequency: Quarterly FDI reports, monthly trade data

sources.name: Controlaría General / ASF (Auditoría Superior de la Federación)
sources.focus: Mexico's federal auditor. Public spending audits, corruption findings, government contract reviews. Essential for detecting misuse of funds and political risk signals.
sources.admiralty: A2 — Autonomous audit body, variable political independence
sources.frequency: Annual audit reports, irregular special reports

sources.name: Fitch/Moody's/S&P — Mexico Sovereign Credit Reports
sources.focus: Mexico sovereign credit ratings, outlook changes, Pemex credit assessments. Essential for sovereign debt risk, investor sentiment.
sources.admiralty: A2 — Major credit rating agencies, methodology-driven

sources.name: Houston Chronicle — Border/Mexico
sources.focus: Texas-based coverage of Mexico border issues: cartels, energy (Pemex/Deer Park), trade, migration. Strong on Texas energy-Mexico nexus.
sources.admiralty: B2 — Regional newspaper with specialized coverage

sources.name: AP News — Mexico
sources.focus: Associated Press Mexico bureau. Breaking news coverage of major cartel incidents, political developments, natural disasters. Wire service baseline.
sources.admiralty: A1 — Major wire service, professional standards

sources.name: Reuters — Mexico
sources.focus: Reuters Mexico bureau. Financial markets, cartel violence, political developments, energy sector, trade. Strong economic/financial coverage.
sources.admiralty: A1 — Major wire service, professional standards

sources.name: Mexican Pacific League/Liga MX — Security Advisories
sources.focus: Sports security for World Cup 2026 venue cities. Liga MX security incidents, stadium safety records, cartel-extortion of clubs. Relevant to worldcup_travel theme.
sources.admiralty: C3 — Sports league, not a security source. Useful for venue-specific incident tracking.

sources.name: CENAPI (Centro Nacional de Planeación de Protección Civil)
sources.focus: National civil protection planning. Natural disaster response capacity, emergency management infrastructure. Relevant to worldcup_travel for venue safety assessment.
sources.admiralty: A2 — Official government civil protection

sources.name: WOLA (Washington Office on Latin America)
sources.focus: Human rights-focused Latin America policy. Mexico migration, drug policy reform, security sector accountability, US-Mexico border rights.
sources.admiralty: A2 — Established human rights think tank, rigorous advocacy research

sources.name: Mexicanos Contra la Corrupción y la Impunidad (MCCI)
sources.focus: Anti-corruption investigative organization. Documented government corruption, conflict of interest, Pemex scandals, campaign finance violations. Forensic investigative work.
sources.admiralty: A2 — Rigorous investigative NGO, multiple award-winning projects

sources.name: Tlachinollan Human Rights Center (Guerrero)
sources.focus: Guerrero-based human rights organization. Forced disappearances, mass graves, cartel violence documentation, community defense. Essential for Guerrero ground truth.
sources.admiralty: B2 — Advocacy NGO, methodical documentation, but advocacy-driven

sources.name: LinkedIn — Mexico Security Community
sources.focus: Follow Mexico security analysts, retired officials, academics on LinkedIn for commentary and signal. Lower-barrier monitoring of professional discourse.
sources.admiralty: C3 — Unstructured professional social media, high noise-to-signal

sources.name: Anabel Hernández (DW/Investigative)
sources.focus: Sinaloa Cartel, Chapitos, El Chapo, government-cartel collusion. Author of multiple books on Mexico drug war. DW investigative journalist.
sources.admiralty: A1 — Leading investigative journalist on Mexican cartels, multi-award-winning, book-length treatment of CDS

sources.name: Jorge Alejandro Medellín (El Universal/Milenio)
sources.focus: Mexican security, SEDENA operations, cartel-military confrontation, defense policy. Longtime security columnist.
sources.admiralty: A2 — Veteran security journalist, knowledgeable about military/cartel operations

sources.name: Small Wars Journal — Mexico
sources.focus: Security analysis of Mexican cartel conflicts, military operations, IED/insurgency patterns. Deep analytical articles.
sources.admiralty: A2 — Professional military studies journal, peer-reviewed analytical articles

sources.name: María Idalia Gómez
sources.focus: Organized crime, cartel atomization, security analysis. Co-author of studies on criminal restructuring.
sources.admiralty: B2 — Established journalist covering organized crime, cited in Infobae and other outlets

sources.name: WSJ Mexico Bureau
sources.focus: Wall Street Journal Mexico coverage: cartels, trade, USMCA, energy, politics. Strong CJNG post-Mencho reporting.
sources.admiralty: A1 — Top-tier financial newspaper, strong editorial standards

sources.name: FT Mexico
sources.focus: Financial Times Mexico coverage: trade, finance, politics, nearshoring, energy policy.
sources.admiralty: A1 — Top-tier financial newspaper, strong Mexico bureau

sources.name: Emeequis Magazine
sources.focus: Mexican investigative magazine. In-depth reporting on cartels, political corruption, security. Spanish-language.
sources.admiralty: B2 — Investigative magazine with variable editorial standards but good ground reporting

sources.name: La Silla Rota
sources.focus: Independent Mexican news website. Security, politics, cartels, US-Mexico relations.
sources.admiralty: B2 — Independent digital outlet, some strong investigative work, variable quality
